content.text: A success criterion is eval-linked when it specifies not only a numeric threshold (the quality bar) but also the exact automated test that will measure it. Without the test specification, the criterion is unverifiable: the team cannot know whether the quality bar has been met before deployment, and regressions in production go undetected until users report them.

The five-step pipeline connects a stakeholder-agreed quality bar to a CI gate that runs on every code change: quality bar → eval set → scorer → gate → CI integration. Each step has a defined owner and a defined artefact. Skipping any step leaves a gap where quality can decay without detection.

attrs.alt: Eval-linked success criteria pipeline: from quality bar to CI gate

content.text: Threshold set without business justification — arbitrary number
Eval set built from synthetic data; doesn't reflect production distribution
Automated judge (LLM-judge, regex, or human sample)
Scorer agreement with human labels not validated before use
Gate exists but is advisory only — team deploys anyway on fail
Eval only runs manually before releases, not on every change

fnol_criteria = [
    SuccessCriterion(
        name="hallucination_rate",
        description="Fraction of policy factual claims not supported by cited source",
        quality_bar=0.02,
        direction="lower_better",
        scorer_type=ScorerType.LLM_JUDGE,
        eval_set_size=100,
        owner="AI Quality Team",
        ci_blocking=True,
    ),
    SuccessCriterion(
        name="intent_accuracy",
        description="Fraction of user messages correctly classified by intent",
        quality_bar=0.94,
        direction="higher_better",
        scorer_type=ScorerType.LLM_JUDGE,
        eval_set_size=200,
        owner="AI Quality Team",
        ci_blocking=True,
    ),
    SuccessCriterion(
        name="pii_in_output_rate",
        description="Fraction of outputs containing personal data not provided by the user",
        quality_bar=0.0,
        direction="lower_better",
        scorer_type=ScorerType.REGEX,
        eval_set_size=500,
        owner="Information Security",
        ci_blocking=True,
    ),
    SuccessCriterion(
        name="citation_coverage",
        description="Fraction of factual claims that include a source reference",
        quality_bar=0.98,
        direction="higher_better",
        scorer_type=ScorerType.REGEX,
        eval_set_size=100,
        owner="AI Quality Team",
        ci_blocking=False,      # advisory, not blocking
    ),
    SuccessCriterion(
        name="refusal_false_positive_rate",
        description="Fraction of legitimate queries incorrectly refused",
        quality_bar=0.03,
        direction="lower_better",
        scorer_type=ScorerType.HUMAN_SAMPLE,
        eval_set_size=100,
        owner="Product",
        ci_blocking=False,
    ),
]

The LLM-judge scorer sends each eval case through the production model, then asks a second model to evaluate the output against the ground truth. It is the standard approach for open-ended text quality evaluation where regex is insufficient.

import openai, json

JUDGE_PROMPT = """
You are evaluating an AI assistant's response for hallucination.

User query: {query}
Assistant response: {response}
Source document excerpt: {source}

Determine whether the assistant made any factual claim about the source material
that is NOT supported by the provided source excerpt.

Respond with JSON only:
{{"hallucinated": true|false, "unsupported_claim": "<quote or null>", "confidence": 0.0–1.0}}
"""

def score_hallucination(
    eval_cases: list[EvalCase],
    get_response: Callable[[str], str],
    source_docs: dict[str, str],
) -> EvalResult:
    client = openai.OpenAI()
    failures = []

    for case in eval_cases:
        response = get_response(case.input)
        source   = source_docs.get(case.context or "", "")

        verdict = json.loads(
            client.chat.completions.create(
                model="gpt-4o-mini",
                response_format={"type": "json_object"},
                messages=[{"role": "user", "content": JUDGE_PROMPT.format(
                    query=case.input, response=response, source=source[:2000]
                )}],
            ).choices[0].message.content
        )

        if verdict["hallucinated"] and verdict["confidence"] >= 0.8:
            failures.append(case.case_id)

    score = len(failures) / len(eval_cases)

    return EvalResult(
        criterion=fnol_criteria[0],   # hallucination_rate criterion
        score=score,
        failing_cases=failures,
        run_id="run-20240701-001",
    )

# Usage in CI:
# result = score_hallucination(eval_set, chatbot.respond, policy_docs)
# print(result.summary())
# if result.criterion.ci_blocking and not result.passed:
#     sys.exit(1)

Validate the judge before trusting it as a gate: run it against 200 cases that have human labels and measure judge-human agreement. If agreement is below 80%, the judge's verdicts are unreliable and will produce both false blocks (wasted engineering time) and false passes (regressions reach production).
When to use human sample instead
When judge agreement < 80% on calibration set
Always prefer regex — deterministic and fast
Human sample for quarterly calibration check
Human sample for any score within 2% of quality bar
Not suitable for LLM-judge — requires contextual judgement

The eval set is the most important artefact in the eval-linked pipeline. A poorly composed eval set produces misleading scores: a model that fails on real production traffic but passes the eval set is worse than no eval at all — it creates false confidence. Compose the set to match the production query distribution, not the easy cases.

Maintain the eval set as a versioned artefact. When production failures occur that the eval set did not catch, add them as regression cases. The eval set should grow monotonically over the lifetime of the feature; cases are never removed, only annotated as 'superseded' if the product scope changes.

A quality bar is a business decision, not a technical one. The AI team can propose a number and explain its measurement, but the threshold must be agreed by the stakeholders who will live with the consequences of false positives (blocked deployments) and false negatives (production failures).

# Quality bar negotiation: documented decision record

QUALITY_BAR_RECORD = {
    "criterion": "hallucination_rate",
    "proposed_bar": 0.02,
    "proposed_by": "AI Quality Team",
    "date": "2024-06-01",

    "business_justification": (
        "A 2% hallucination rate on 10,000 weekly FNOL interactions produces ~200 incorrect "
        "policy coverage statements per week. At 8% rework rate on incorrect statements, "
        "that is 16 claims requiring human correction — within the 20-claim weekly capacity "
        "reserved for AI error remediation."
    ),

    "stakeholder_sign_offs": {
        "Claims Operations Director": "Accepted — aligns with remediation capacity",
        "Information Security":       "Accepted — not a data-leak vector",
        "Legal":                      "Conditional — requires citation on every factual claim",
        "Product Owner":              "Accepted",
    },

    "alternative_considered": {
        "bar": 0.05,
        "rejected_because": "5% would produce 500 incorrect statements/week, exceeding remediation capacity",
    },

    "review_trigger": "Re-negotiate if weekly FNOL volume exceeds 20,000 (error count doubles)",
}

Document the business justification for every quality bar. Without it, the threshold will be renegotiated under pressure after the first blocked deployment — and it will be renegotiated upward, making the gate easier to pass rather than improving the model.

Not every criterion should block deployment. Classify gates explicitly: blocking gates prevent deployment when failed; advisory gates report the score and alert the owner but permit deployment. Misclassifying advisory gates as blocking leads to alert fatigue; misclassifying blocking gates as advisory allows quality regressions to reach production.
Deployment stops; owner alerted; root cause required
Quality metrics not in hot path
New fairness probe scores during pilot

A single eval run answers 'did this version pass?' Regression detection answers 'is quality trending in the wrong direction?' A model that passes the threshold every week but whose scores worsen from 1.2% to 1.7% to 1.9% is approaching a breach that will surface as a production failure, not a CI failure.

Run trend detection on every eval run. Alert the owner when a metric shows monotonic worsening over five consecutive runs, even if still above the quality bar. This allows root cause investigation before the threshold is breached.

The production query distribution shifts over time: new claim types appear, user vocabulary changes, edge cases surface that were not anticipated at launch. An eval set that was representative at launch becomes increasingly unrepresentative over 6–12 months.
New claim types added to scope
Ops team adds new FNOL category
Add 20 cases for new category; rebalance composition
Production failure not caught by eval
Human QA finds error type absent from eval set
All weekly scores within 0.2% of threshold
Refresh eval set with harder cases from production sample
Eval scores shift without prompt change
Run eval before and after update; add any new failure types as cases
Retire superseded cases; add 20–30 fresh cases from production logs

Schedule a quarterly eval set review as a formal artefact alongside the metric framework review. The reviewer compares the eval set composition against the last 90 days of production query distribution and adds cases for any category underrepresented by more than 5 percentage points.
Connecting Eval Gates to the Deployment Pipeline
Lightweight eval (50 cases, blocking criteria only)
PR marked as failing; engineer investigates
Full eval set (100–500 cases, all criteria)
Deployment to staging blocked; owner alerted
Score compared to baseline; alert if > 0.5σ drift
Automatic rollback if blocking criterion fails within 1 h
Update held if any blocking criterion fails
Quality bar set without business justification
Renegotiated after first block; gate becomes ineffective
Document error-volume calculation that justifies the number
Eval set built from synthetic data only
Seed with real production interactions from day 1
Validate judge on 200 human-labelled cases before using as a gate
Classify PII, safety, and core accuracy gates as blocking
Production distribution drifts; scores stop predicting failures
Quarterly review; add regression cases immediately after any production failure
Eval only runs before major releases
Regressions introduced by prompt or dependency changes go undetected for weeks
Run eval on every PR; at minimum on every merge to main

Eval-linked success criteria connect a stakeholder-agreed quality bar to an automated CI gate through five steps: define the numeric threshold with business justification, compose a representative eval set, implement and calibrate a scorer, configure the pass/fail gate with appropriate blocking classification, and integrate into CI so every code change is evaluated. The quality bar is a business negotiation, not a technical choice — document the error-volume calculation that justifies it. The eval set is the most important artefact: it must reflect the production query distribution, grow with regression cases from production failures, and be reviewed quarterly. Regression trend detection catches slow-burn quality decay before thresholds are breached.
